title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Impact of Tourism on Historical Sites

texts.A: The ostensible preservation of ancient monuments is frequently invoked as the primary justification for encouraging mass international tourism, yet the profound irony is that the relentless volume of visitors is itself the principal force accelerating their destruction. [38]The cumulative friction of millions of shuffling footsteps each year, the damaging humidity continuously generated by human breath in enclosed or semi-enclosed spaces, and the persistent unauthorized removal of small fragments all cause irreversible and steadily accelerating physical deterioration.[/38] [40]We should urgently prioritize the development of high-quality virtual reality and augmented reality experiences as the primary and most widely accessible means through which people 'visit' the world's most fragile and irreplaceable historical monuments.[/40] [37]Rigorous physical access should be permanently restricted to a carefully vetted and numerically limited group of accredited scholars, conservation professionals, and qualified researchers.[/37] A monument that is quite literally loved to physical destruction is a monument that we as a civilization have fundamentally and shamefully failed to protect. Our admiration must learn the discipline of restraint.
texts.B: To permanently restrict access to humanity's most significant historical and cultural sites to a privileged class of professional 'scholars' is a deeply elitist and socially regressive form of institutional gatekeeping that effectively denies ordinary people their rightful connection to their shared human heritage and collective history. [39]Tourism provides the essential and frequently irreplaceable economic engine that makes large-scale conservation work financially viable in many developing and cash-constrained nations where alternative funding sources are entirely insufficient.[/39] Without the consistent and substantial revenue generated from visitor entry fees, local hospitality services, and broader economic activity, many of these precious sites would rapidly deteriorate further or be lost permanently to commercial looters and well-organized criminal networks. [37]The genuine challenge is therefore not to prohibit meaningful public access, but to invest creatively and adequately in innovative crowd management techniques and long-term structural site reinforcement programs.[/37] A monument permanently invisible and inaccessible to the public will quickly be forgotten by the very taxpayers whose governments are expected to fund its ongoing conservation.
texts.C: [38]I share the genuine and urgent view that the accelerating physical toll inflicted on ancient monuments by poorly managed mass tourism represents a real conservation crisis requiring immediate action, but I fundamentally disagree that virtual reality technology offers a philosophically adequate or emotionally satisfying long-term solution.[/38] [40]A digital reproduction, however technically impressive and visually accurate, inevitably lacks the irreplaceable 'aura' of physical authenticity and utterly fails to provide the profound historical resonance and emotional connection that can only genuinely be experienced through direct physical presence at a site of real antiquity.[/40] However, I agree entirely that the dominant 'bucket-list' model of rapid, superficial tourism—driven primarily by social media metrics and the compulsive collection of experiences—is both ecologically and culturally unsustainable. We urgently need a fundamental conceptual shift toward 'slow tourism,' where visitors are required to spend significantly more time in each region, contribute meaningfully to local conservation efforts, and engage deeply and respectfully with living communities.
texts.D: The contemporary debate over historical site preservation too frequently overlooks the fundamental human question of the role and rights of the local community in the entire conservation process. Far too often, significant historical and archaeological sites are progressively transformed into sterile, tightly controlled 'museum bubbles' from which the long-established original inhabitants are physically displaced and economically marginalized to make way for tourist infrastructure, luxury hotels, and international visitor management systems. This process transforms vibrant, living history into a commodified and sanitized product manufactured for external consumption and Instagram photographs. While I genuinely share the concern about ongoing physical erosion of these irreplaceable sites, I believe the deeper and more consequential danger is the systematic erosion of their authentic social context and the irreplaceable living memory embedded in the surrounding communities. [39]We must thoughtfully integrate conservation priorities with genuine local community development, ensuring that people living near these sites become their primary guardians and principal economic beneficiaries, not merely low-wage service workers.[/39]

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the best way to handle visitor access to fragile sites?
questions.explanation: Expert B wants to 'innovate in crowd management' but keep access open. Expert A argues physical access should be 'restricted to a tiny number of scholars' and replaced with virtual reality.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the extent of the physical damage caused by visitors?
questions.explanation: Expert A mentions 'irreversible damage' from footsteps and breath. Expert C also agrees that the 'physical toll on monuments is a crisis.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the impact of tourism on the local people around a site?
questions.explanation: Expert D talks about the 'local community' and people being 'guardians.' Expert B also emphasizes that tourism provides 'vital infrastructure and income' to 'mountain regions' (or local regions), agreeing on the economic link.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on the value of digital alternatives to physical travel?
questions.explanation: Expert C argues digital copies lack 'aura' and are not the answer. Expert A argues that we should 'prioritize virtual reality experiences' as the primary way to visit sites.
